parsed.summary: This commit bundles an interactive menu for /glm:setup re-configuration with an npm scope rename. The scope rename (likely changing the package scope from one name to another) is a high-impact operational change that could break existing installations, CI pipelines, and published package references. The setup command changes introduce interactive prompting behavior that may not handle non-interactive/TUI-constrained environments gracefully. These two concerns — a scope rename mixed with feature work in a single commit — create risk of silent breakage for existing users.

findings.title: npm scope rename bundled with feature work risks breaking existing installs
findings.body: The commit message explicitly mentions 'rename npm scope' alongside the interactive menu feature. A scope rename in package.json means any existing installation referencing the old scoped package name will fail to resolve after this change. This includes: pinned references in other projects, CI caches, lockfiles, and any documentation or scripts referencing the old scope. Bundling this breaking change with unrelated feature work in a single commit makes rollback and bisecting harder — if the menu change has a bug, reverting also reverts the scope rename, and vice versa. There is no evidence of a migration path, deprecation notice, or compatibility shim for the old scope name.
findings.recommendation: Separate the npm scope rename into its own commit with an accompanying migration plan. Ensure the old scoped package either continues to publish as a redirect/alias or that a clear deprecation and migration guide is provided. Verify that no CI/CD pipelines, documentation, or downstream consumers reference the old scope.

findings.title: Interactive menu in setup command may fail or hang in non-interactive environments
findings.body: The setup command now presents an interactive menu when already configured. If this command is invoked in a non-interactive context (CI, scripting, piped input), the prompt may hang indefinitely waiting for user input, or produce unexpected behavior. There is no visible guard for detecting TTY availability or a --yes/--force flag to skip the interactive prompt. This can silently block automation pipelines.
findings.recommendation: Add a TTY detection guard or a non-interactive fallback (e.g., exit with status 0 and a message, or accept a flag like --reconfigure to explicitly opt into the interactive flow). Document the expected behavior in headless/CI environments.

next_steps: Inspect the full diff of package.json to confirm the old and new scope names and assess blast radius of the rename.
Check if commands/setup.md contains any TTY detection, timeout, or non-interactive mode handling in the prompt logic.
Verify whether the .claude-plugin/plugin.json and marketplace.json changes are consistent with the new scope name and don't reference stale identifiers.
Consider splitting this into two commits: one for the scope rename (with migration docs) and one for the interactive menu feature.
